coredata.dc:title: Tailoring the hydrogen storage performance of the Cr-, Mn-, and Fe-doped circumcoronenes by the presence of N and B co-dopants: Computational study

coredata.dc:description: Reliance of human population on quickly depleting fossil fuels stimulates a search for sustainable alternatives. Hydrogen represents one of the most promising candidates to replace the largely consumed fossil fuels. One of the crucial issues of the hydrogen cycle is the H2 gas storage under ambient conditions. Hence, a search for effective H2 storage devices is of urge importance. The H2 storage capacity of the Cr-, Mn-, and Fe-doped circumcoronenes (CCs) in the presence of N and B co-dopants is investigated at the DFT level of theory. Obtained results suggest that the presence of N co-dopants significantly enhances the affinity of the studied systems towards H2 binding, which consequently leads to improved H2 storage properties. Moreover, the presence of three N co-dopants energetically stabilizes the Cr-, Mn-, and Fe-doped CCs in their low-spin states, which are the most suitable for the H2 adsorption. In addition, the H2 adsorption performance of the Mn–3N-doped CCs can be altered by the oxidation and reduction. This finding indicates that an application of the external electric potential may control the H2 release-and-capture mechanism in Mn-doped CC storage devices. In addition, the metal-doped CC cation appears as the energetically preferred form (metal-doped CC is a strong reducing agent when compared to the ferrocene couple).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Reliance of human population on quickly depleting fossil fuels stimulates a search for sustainable alternatives. Hydrogen represents one of the most promising candidates to replace the largely consumed fossil fuels. One of the crucial issues of the hydrogen cycle is the H2 gas storage under ambient conditions. Hence, a search for effective H2 storage devices is of urge importance. The H2 storage capacity of the Cr-, Mn-, and Fe-doped circumcoronenes (CCs) in the presence of N and B co-dopants is investigated at the DFT level of theory. Obtained results suggest that the presence of N co-dopants significantly enhances the affinity of the studied systems towards H2 binding, which consequently leads to improved H2 storage properties. Moreover, the presence of three N co-dopants energetically stabilizes the Cr-, Mn-, and Fe-doped CCs in their low-spin states, which are the most suitable for the H2 adsorption. In addition, the H2 adsorption performance of the Mn–3N-doped CCs can be altered by the oxidation and reduction. This finding indicates that an application of the external electric potential may control the H2 release-and-capture mechanism in Mn-doped CC storage devices. In addition, the metal-doped CC cation appears as the energetically preferred form (metal-doped CC is a strong reducing agent when compared to the ferrocene couple).

ce:section.ce:para: Reliance on fossil fuels is one of the most excessive problems of the present days. Rapidly growing human population puts extreme demands on Earth's energy sources, resulting in the so-called energy crisis [1]. Hence, finding a way to replace the quickly depleting fossil fuels with renewable energy resources is of utter importance. Such alternatives are e.g. solar energy [2], wind energy [3], or hydrogen energy [4]. Hydrogen gas is one of the most promising “green” energy resources due to its outstanding energy carrier properties and high energy yield [4]. However, to fulfill the criteria of being the source of green energy, hydrogen must be produced in an environmentally friendly way [5–8]. Further issue which has to be consistently solved within the hydrogen energy cycle is the efficient storage of hydrogen gas [4,9,10]. The hydrogen gas (H2) storage under ambient condition is problematic due to a low energy density of H2 molecules [11]. Current standard for H2 storage is based solely on the high-pressure tanks (350 or 700 bar) [12] at least in the fuel cell electric vehicles. The 2025 storage systems should have the kg H2/kg system parameter equal to 5.5 wt % [13]. Storing hydrogen at high pressure is dangerous and thus more convenient techniques are desirable. Metal hydrides are one of the options, although kinetics is less favoring (additional bottlenecks are either temperatures above 300 °C, or lower wt % of H2 to storage material and/or the price of metals beyond 3d elements) [11,14]. Liquid-organic hydrogen carriers (LOHCs) are organic substances that are able to store hydrogen by catalytic hydrogenation and dehydrogenation processes [15,16]. However, it is well-known that dehydrogenation is a highly endothermic process and, in general, high temperature and catalysts are required. Additional question arises with respect to the number of cycles until chemical changes occur in the LOHC solvent (H2 chemisorbing) molecules. Another class of compounds are the metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), which are designed with the perspective application as cryogenic (77 K) temperatures H2 storage materials [10,17–19]. Adsorption and desorption of hydrogen gas at ambient conditions represent an energetically more feasible alternative. The essential step in the storage process is the adsorption of host molecules (in this case H2) on the particular adsorbent [9,10,20]. Carbon-based nanomaterials have already found their potential utilization as adsorbents of gas molecules [21], H2 storage materials [22], or water-splitting devices [23,24]. It has been shown that the pristine graphene surface (GS) [25] is able to physisorb H2 molecules [26]. Such interaction is not sufficient for an effective H2 storage (the predicted physisorption energy is approximately −5 kJ mol−1 [26]), but chemical modification of the GS may significantly increase its H2 adsorption capability [27–29]. One of the most common methods to modify the GS is doping with heteroatoms (replacing a carbon atom with a dopant atom), usually transition metals [30–37]. The adsorption capacity of such modified GSs is found to be three H2 molecules on one transition metal (e.g. Os [27], Cr [28], Li [30,38], Cu [34], Fe [36]). Despite the huge progress made in the last years, the search and design of novel and more effective H2 storage devices is still an ongoing and utter topic.

In one of our previous works [36] we have investigated the ability to adsorb H2 molecules in a series of graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which were used as model GS systems, in particular coronenes and circumcoronenes (CCs) doped with different transition metals (TMs). These GQDs are two-dimensional structures derived from graphene with a size up to 100 nm [39]. GQDs doped with Cr, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, and Zn atoms with different multiplicities have been theoretically studied [36]. It has been shown that the Cr-, Mn-, and Fe-doped GQDs can be used as promising H2 adsorbents with the limit of three H2 molecules per one TM center [36]. Based on our previous results, these three transition metals (Cr, Mn, and Fe) have been selected as dopant atoms for an improved H2 adsorption site.

In the presented work, the impact of the presence of N and B co-dopants on the H2 adsorption ability of Cr-, Mn-, and Fe-doped circumcoronene (CC) is examined. CC, chosen as a model system of the GQD, is a polyaromatic hydrocarbon composed of 54 carbon atoms arranged in 19 hexagonal rings [40], and N and B are among the most widely used heteroatom dopants in graphene based materials [41–45]. Positive effect of the N co-dopants on the H2 adsorption ability of the Cu-, and Au-doped GQDs has been already pointed out by Singla and Jaggi [46,47]. They have reported that an addition of one N atom into the structure of Au-doped GQD leads to a shortening of Au–H2 binding distance from 1.99 Å to 1.83 Å [47]. In addition, the Fe-containing N-doped carbon surfaces have already been successfully synthesized [48] and they proved to be also efficient oxygen and nitrogen reducing agents [49,50]. The formation possibilities of such modified GQDs can be evaluated via their calculated formation energies [49,51]. The first goal of this study is to compare the H2 adsorption ability of the modified CCs and to further explore the most suitable options for H2 storage. The second goal of the presented study is to investigate the effect of charging and discharging of these systems on their H2 storage performance. Since the doping of CCs with TMs tunes their conductivity [52], and related properties, it is possible to effectively control the H2 storage by applying an external electric potential [53].

Optimized geometries of the Cr-, Mn-, and Fe-doped CC were taken from authors' previous work [36]. Additional nitrogen or boron dopant atoms were placed in the neighboring positions of the TM atom. Positions of the dopant atoms and labeling of the systems under study are shown in Scheme 1 . These systems were optimized using the B3LYP [54–57] functional including the D3 version of Grimme's dispersion correction (GD3) [58] and 6-311G∗∗ basis set [59,60]. B3LYP-GD3 computational protocol has already proved its suitability to treat weak intermolecular interactions in the vicinity of graphene surfaces [61–63]. The use of 6-311G∗∗ basis set in the calculations of GQDs provides reproducible results at reasonable computational cost [36]. For completeness, optimized structures of all systems from Scheme 1 are compiled in Figure S1.

Subsequently, three interacting H2 molecules were placed over the TM dopant atom in all the studied systems. The initial distance between the H2 molecules and TM was approximately 2.2 Å. The number of H2 molecules (three) corresponds to the storage capacity of one TM atom on the graphene surface [27,28,30,34,36]. Systems including H2 molecules were re-optimized using the same computational protocol as mentioned above (B3LYP-GD3/6-311G∗∗). All geometry optimizations were carried out using the Gaussian16 program package [64] and the default settings of the calculation parameters were employed, if not stated otherwise. The SCF convergence was set to tight [65], however, in some high-spin state cases, quadratically convergent (QC) SCF procedure [66] had to be employed.

The basis set superposition error (BSSE) corrected E ads adsorption energies between the doped CC and the H2 molecules were calculated using the equation:
(1)
E ads = E CC/3H2 – (E CC + E 3H2 )
where E CC/3H2 is the energy of system with adsorbed H2 molecules, E CC and E 3H2 are the BSSE corrected energies of the doped system and three H2 molecules, respectively, i.e. BSSE correction is performed via the counterpoise scheme of Boys and Bernardi [67]. Molekel software suite [68] was used for visualization of the calculated structures.

The QTAIM analysis [69] as implemented in the AIMAll package [70] was performed for chosen systems using the Gaussian16 checkpoint files. The QTAIM analysis was employed to obtain atomic charge and spin populations, and to estimate the character and strength of the TM-H bonds using delocalization indexes (DI) [71].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrogen binding performance: spin state preference, TM-H distances, adsorption energies
ce:section.ce:para:
Relative energies (ΔE) of the studied electroneutral systems in different spin states are presented in Tables 1–3
. The energetically most preferred spin state (i.e. lowest in energy) of every studied system is set in bold. The calculated TM-H distances (d) and adsorption energies (E ads) are shown in Tables 1–3, as well. The shortest TM-H bonds and the most negative E ads values, corresponding to the strongest TM-H2 binding, for every system under study are set in bold. Optimized structures of the studied systems in their most stable spin states including three adsorbed H2 molecules are compiled in Figs. 1 and 2
. Three H2 molecules interact with TM atom in a side-on way (see Figure S2), forming η2-dihydrogen bonds [72,73] in all the studied cases. For completeness, side views of the optimized systems including adsorbed H2 molecules are shown in Figures S3 and S4. Calculated TM-N and TM-B bond distances for the selected systems are presented in Table S1.

As can be seen from Table 1, doping the CC-Cr structure with one N atom enhances their H2 binding performance (the calculated E ads value increases from −80 to −100 kJ mol−1). Moreover, doping with three N atoms increases the H2 binding performance even further (the calculated E ads value is −228 kJ mol−1). The more negative adsorption energies are also reflected in the calculated Cr–H distances. In the case of the CC-3N-Cr, all three H2 molecules bind to the Cr atom in a side-on way, and the Cr–H distances are around 1.74 Å, which is significantly shorter than the ones in the Cr-CC system (ranging from 1.88 Å to 2.33 Å). In addition, the presence of three N atoms in the CC-Cr structure energetically stabilizes the low-spin state (doublet), which is also the one with the best H2 adsorption capacity. All three calculated Cr–N distances in this system are 2.02 Å, see Table S1. One can also notice that, in the case of CC-N-Cr, the quartet spin state is energetically preferred over the doublet, but the stronger H2 binding ability is found for the doublet. A similar finding has been reported in our earlier study for the Cr-doped GQDs [36].

On the other hand, doping the CC-Cr structure with one B atom does not improve the H2 binding ability of the system (the calculated E ads value is decreased from −80 to −55 kJ mol−1). Contrary to that, doping of the CC-Cr structure with three B atoms enhances its H2 binding performance, but the calculated E ads value (being −133 kJ mol−1) is still significantly less negative than the one of CC-3N-Cr (being −228 kJ mol−1). Additionally, the obtained ΔE ads value of −133 kJ mol−1 correspond to the low-spin state (doublet) which is energetically not preferred. Sextet is the energetically preferred spin state in the case of CC-3B-Cr, see Table 1. However, this spin state leads to longer Cr–B bond distances, pointing out a destabilization of the Cr atom over the CC-3B backbone, see Table S1.

As can be seen from Table 2, the H2 binding ability of the Mn-doped systems has similar trends as in the Cr-doped CCs. The shortest Mn–H distances (around 1.66 Å) as well as the most negative E ads value (being −297 kJ mol−1) is found in the case of CC-3N-Mn. The calculated Mn–N distances in this system are 2.01 Å, see Table S1. Only a slightly weaker H2 adsorption is found in the case of CC-3B-Mn (Mn–H distances of ca. 1.70 Å and adsorption energy of −267 kJ mol−1). Interestingly, doping of the CC-Mn with three B atoms leads to a destabilization of the Mn dopant atom over the CC-3B backbone, see Mn–B distances in Table S1. Herein, the Mn atom is bound only with two N atoms, which results in the perturbation of the originally regular honeycomb CC structure, as can be seen in Fig. 2d and S1. These perturbations are found only in the case of the CC-3B-Mn, and they are not further affected by the adsorption of H2 molecules (cf. Fig. 2d and S1).

Contrary to the case of the Cr-doped CCs (Table 1), doping with one N or B atom already leads to improved H2 adsorption when compared to the Mn-doped CC (E ads increased from ca. −80 to ca. −120 kJ mol−1). However, the energetically most stable spin state (singlet) of CC-3N-Mn is also showing the best H2 binding performance, see Table 2. In the case of the CC-N-Mn, CC-B-Mn, and CC-3B-Mn systems, the most negative E ads values are obtained for the low-spin state (singlet), but the higher spin states (triplet or pentet) are energetically preferred.

For comparison, a co-existence of N and B co-dopants in the vicinity of Mn atom is considered, see Table S2. However, these systems show worse H2 adsorption performance when compared to the CC-3N-Mn or CC-3B-Mn systems, as can be seen from the calculated Mn–H distances and adsorption energies (E ads values are around −140 kJ mol−1) compiled in Table S2.

In the case of the Fe-doped CCs, the trends in the H2 binding performance are rather different than in their Cr and Mn analogs, see Table 3. Herein, an enhanced ability to bind H2 molecules is found only in the case of the CC-3N-Fe (Fe–H distances of ca. 1.75 Å and adsorption energy of −152 kJ mol−1). The calculated Fe–N distances in this system are around 1.92 Å, see Table S1. The calculated E ads values for the rest of the systems (CC–N–Fe, CC-B-Fe, and CC-3B-Fe) are ranging from −76 to −78 kJ mol−1, which is less negative than the E ads value of the reference CC-Fe system (−98 kJ mol−1). As for the spin state preference, the most stable spin state correspond to the strongest H2 binding in all of the Fe-doped CCs, see Table 3. These findings are in agreement with the previously reported results related to the Fe-doped GQDs [36].

In summary, the presence of one N dopant in the vicinity of TM within the CC structure positively affects the H2 adsorption in the case of the electroneutral Cr-, and Mn-doped systems. On the other hand, the presence of one B dopant in the CC structure improves the H2 binding performance only in the case of the Mn-doped CC. However, for all the studied electroneutral systems (Cr-, Mn-, Fe-doped), the best H2 binding performance can be achieved by doping the CC-TM with three N atoms. The presence of these three N atoms leads to a shorter (by 0.2 Å), and hence stronger, TM-H bond than in the case of CC-TM. Naturally, the highly electronegative N atoms have electron-withdrawing effect on the particular TM, which is consequently capable of the stronger H2 adsorption. Furthermore, the low-spin state is energetically stabilized in the systems containing three N atoms. This aspect is also important, since the low-spin state is the most suitable for H2 adsorption (i.e., most negative E ads values) [36]. For comparison, doping with three B atoms enhances the H2 adsorption in the case of the Cr-, and Mn-doped systems relative to the reference CC-TM. However, in the case of the CC-3B-Mn, a destabilization of the Mn-dopant over the CC-3B backbone is found, see Fig. 2d. Finally, doping with (one or three) B atoms decreases the ability to bind H2 molecules in the case of the Fe-doped CCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Oxidation/reduction of the CC-3N-TM: the H2 storage and the release-and-capture cycle
ce:section.ce:para: The main aim of this part of the manuscript is to examine whether the H2 binding performance of the CC-3N-TM can be influenced by the application of an external electric potential, i.e., change of the total charge. In this respect, several aspects have to be taken into account. First of all, conductivity of the studied material has to be considered. Conductivity of a solid material is directly linked to its energy band gap. Calculated LUMO–HOMO band gaps (ΔE LUMO–HOMO) of the CC-3N-TM species with different charge (q) are presented in Table S3. All the ΔE LUMO–HOMO values are within the range of 0.8–2.4 eV, which indicates that the studied CC-3N-TM species behave like semiconductors [52,74,75] and consequently are oxidation/reduction accessible. Herein, the CC-3N-TM systems are considered in this section because these were found to be the most effective H2 storage materials (see Tables 1–3). Second important issue, which has to be considered, is the energy difference between the oxidized/reduced systems and the neutral one, representing the energy barrier which is required for such oxidation/reduction process. Oxidation/reduction energies (ΔE Redox) and the calculated adsorption energies (E ads) of the CC-3N-TM species with different charge (q) are compiled in Table S3 including different spin states. The spin state preference is shown in Table S4. For completeness, the calculated TM-H and H–H bond distances, corresponding delocalization indexes (DI) values, as well as partial QTAIM charges on TM and H2 molecules of the differently charged CC-3N-TM + 3H2 species are presented in Tables S5-S10.

The impact of CC-3N-Cr oxidation/reduction on the H2 adsorption energy is depicted in Fig. 3 (for particular values see Table S3). As can be seen from Fig. 3, one-electron oxidation of the electroneutral CC-3N-Cr (q = 0) leads to a considerably weaker H2 adsorption (E ads value increases from ca. −228 to ca. −160 kJ mol−1). Energy difference (theoretical redox potential) between the electroneutral (q = 0) and oxidized (q = 1) CC-3N-Cr species is 4.35 eV, when considering the energetically preferred triplet spin state. The oxidation with such an energy barrier would be possible. On the contrary, one-electron reduction of this system slightly strengthens the H2 binding ability (E ads value decreases to ca. −232 kJ mol−1, when considering the energetically preferred triplet spin state). The energy difference between the electroneutral (q = 0) and reduced (q = −1) form of the CC-3N-Cr is only −1.5 eV, which can be more easily accessed than oxidation. The second reduction step leads to a further decrease of the E ads value (−262 kJ mol−1), while the energy difference between the reduced (q = −1) and double-reduced (q = −2) form of CC-3N-Cr is 1.2 eV.

To sum up, the oxidation of the CC-3N-Cr + 3H2 system weakens the Cr–H2 bonding, but not enough to release these H2 molecules. Still, the more easily accessible reduction of the CC-3N-Cr + 3H2 system slightly strengthens the Cr–H2 interaction.

The impact of CC-3N-Mn oxidation/reduction on the H2 adsorption energy is depicted in Fig. 4 (for particular values see Table S3). Similar to CC-3N-Cr, one-electron oxidation of the electroneutral CC-3N-Mn (q = 0) system significantly weakens its H2 adsorption ability. The E ads value increases from ca. −297 to ca. −43 kJ mol−1, when considering the energetically preferred high-spin state (q = 1, M S = 6). The corresponding oxidation energy of ca. 4.2 eV points on the option of such oxidation. The second oxidation of the CC-3N-Mn leads to a similar E ads value (ca. −49 kJ mol−1) when considering the energetically preferred high-spin state (q = 2, M S = 7), however, the energy barrier of this oxidation is two-times higher (8.3 eV) when compared to the first one. Contrary to that, one-electron reduction of the electroneutral CC-3N-Mn significantly strengthens its H2 binding ability. The E ads value decreases to ca. −585 kJ mol−1, when considering the energetically preferred high-spin state (q = −1, M S = 6). Such a huge decrease in the E ads value is caused by the dissociation of one of the H2 molecules, which occurs on the high-spin state CC-3N-Mn system. Note that, in the case of the low-spin state (M S = 2, energetically not favored), three H2 molecules are adsorbed on the Mn atom in a similar side-on way as in the case of the electroneutral CC-3N-Mn (Fig. 1d) and with the E ads value of ca. −273 kJ mol−1. The whole situation is illustrated in Figure S5. In addition, the calculated energy of one-electron reduction of the electroneutral CC-3N-Mn is –2.4 eV, when considering the energetically preferred high-spin state of the reduced species. Hence, such a reduction is still more easily accessible than oxidation.

In conclusion, the oxidation and reduction of the electroneutral CC-3N-Mn + 3H2 system significantly affects its ability to bind H2 molecules. Hence, an application of the external potential may serve as a switch for the H2 release-and-capture mechanism in similar Mn-doped nanomaterials. In addition, one H2 molecule is dissociated under reduction, and this is not only interesting in the perspective of H2 storage, but also with respect to H2 reactivity and electro-catalysis. Nevertheless, it has to be stressed (see footnote to Table S2) that the reaction enthalpy for the single reduced CC-3N-Mn + 3H2 species is −116 kJ mol−1 (same spin state assumed). Hence, the evaluation of E ads considers the dissociated H2 molecule as the reference, which obtains the largely negative interaction energy. While the reaction enthalpy for the electroneutral species is −191 kJ mol−1 (same spin state assumed). Also here, the changes of the H2 bond lengths and the overall geometry leads to an approximately 100 kJ mol−1 smaller reaction enthalpy when compared to E ads. As can be seen from Table S7, the distance between the hydrogen atoms of the dissociated H2 molecule in the single reduced CC-3N-Mn + 3H2 species is 2.15 Å. The physical DI value is 0.10, and no QTAIM bond critical point was found between these two hydrogen atoms. The QTAIM charge on these hydrogens is in total −0.83 e, which is almost ten times more than when an H2 molecule is adsorbed on a CC-TM system. Although the Mn–H bond distance for the single reduced species is only 0.05 Å shorter than in the electroneutral one, the DI value is almost twice as large, see Table S8. The charge on Mn does not change upon the reduction of CC-3N-Mn + 3H2 species. Hence, the reduction occurs at an H2 molecule leading to the CC-3N-[Mn(H)2]- system.

Last but not least, the impact of CC-3N-Fe oxidation/reduction on the H2 adsorption energy is depicted in Fig. 5 (for particular values see Table S3). Contrary to the CC-3N-Cr and CC-3N-Mn, the oxidation of the electroneutral CC-3N-Fe (q = 0) slightly increases its H2 binding ability The calculated E ads values almost linearly decrease with every oxidation step (E ads values are −152, −160, and −168 kJ mol−1 for q = 0, 1, and 2, respectively). On the other hand, one-electron reduction of the electroneutral CC-3N-Fe (q = 0) leads to a significant weakening of its H2 adsorption ability (E ads value increases to ca. −105 kJ mol−1, when considering the energetically preferred high-spin state, M S = 5). The energy barrier of oxidation and reduction is −4.2 eV and −1.6 eV, respectively. Interestingly, the second reduction of this system (q = −2) considerably strengthens its H2 adsorption performance (E ads value decreases to ca. −173 kJ mol−1, when considering the energetically preferred quartet spin state). The energy difference between the reduced (q = −1) and double-reduced (q = −2) forms of CC-3N-Fe is 0.9 eV, when considering the energetically preferred spin states.

To sum up, the reduction of the electroneutral CC-3N-Fe + 3H2 system has a potential to induce the release of H2 molecules, but this system is not as promising for the design of H2 release-and-capture cycle as the Mn-doped one, although it is more promising than CC-3N-Cr.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Relevance of CC-3N-TM charged species in real environment
ce:section.ce:para: When considering the oxidation or reduction theoretically, several other aspects need to be mentioned (charge preference, choice of reference). The ferrocene couple (Fc+/Fc) is taken as the gold standard in electrochemistry in solution [76–78]. The Fc+/Fc couple has the formal in vacuo B3LYP/6-311G∗∗ ΔE Redox value of −7.1 eV. This suggests that CC-3N-TM (consider the ΔE Redox value of ca −4.2 eV) is oxidized to CC-3N-TM+ in the presence of Fc+. Hence, the existence of CC-3N-TM+ is preferred over CC-3N-TM in the presence of a mild redox agent, such as Fc+. The experimental redox potential of the Fc+/Fc couple in acetonitrile versus the saturated calomel electrode is 0.380 V [76,79]. According to these findings, CC-3N-TM appears as a reducing agent when considering the formal in vacuo ΔE Redox values. Let us further note that the electron enthalpy (free energy of electron is −0.04 eV [76,78]) has been neglected in our estimates. In addition, when comparing Fc+/Fc standard potentials obtained upon either the free energies or the total SCF energies (SCF Done) for the acetonitrile polarizable continuum solvent model [80], these redox potentials differ by 0.01 eV. The total SCF energies based Fc+/Fc redox potential in acetonitrile is −5.27 eV and the theoretical reference values are between −4.90 and −5.00 eV [76,78]. Thus, the direct usage of total SCF energies is a very reasonable and cost-efficient choice for the estimation of redox potentials.

In addition, the aforementioned formal CC-3N-TM+/CC-3N-TM ΔE Redox value of around −4.2 eV is well within the electrochemical window (EW) of some commonly used solvents, e.g. acetonitrile [81], or ionic liquids [82]. Still, it should be emphasized that in this work, we consider only the gas phase adsorption of H2, where the EW could be considerably larger (yet not very shifted from the formal in vacuo one) [83]. Under such conditions, redox potential of ±5 eV should not be the limitation for a hydrogen gas capacitator composed of CC-3N-TM units linked directly to electrodes.

The effect of N and B co-dopants on the hydrogen storage ability of the Cr-, Mn-, and Fe-doped circumcoronenes has been investigated at the DFT level of theory. It has been shown that all of the studied electroneutral systems are able to adsorb three H2 molecules via dihapto bonds. Overall, the best H2 binding performance among the studied systems has been achieved in the case of the CC-3N-Mn. The calculated adsorption energy of three H2 molecules on this system is −297 kJ mol−1, which is more than three-times higher when compared to the CC-Mn system (E ads value is approximately −81 kJ mol−1). Similarly, the presence of three N co-dopants in the CC-3N-Cr and the CC-3N-Fe also leads to an increase of their H2 binding performance (E ads values are approximately −228 kJ mol−1 and –152 kJ mol−1, respectively). Such a positive impact on the H2 binding is likely caused by the electron-withdrawing effects of the highly electronegative N atoms, placed in the vicinity of the TM dopant. When considering the H2 to system wt %, in the Mn doped systems for instance, the actual value for a single 3N–Mn nest is 0.8 wt %, utilizing four 3N–Mn nests would reach 2.8 wt %.

On the other hand, the presence of three B co-dopants also enhances the H2 binding performance, but only in the case of the Mn and Cr containing systems (E ads are approx. −267 kJ mol−1 and –133 kJ mol−1, respectively). However, this strengthening of the TM-H2 binding is not as large as in the case of the systems containing three N co-dopants. (The H2 to system wt % in a four 3B–Mn nests system would reach 3.0 wt %.)

The effect of oxidation/reduction of the CC-3N-TM systems on their H2 storage performance has been investigated subsequently. It has been shown that the H2 adsorption on the CC-3N-Mn can be significantly affected by the oxidation and/or reduction of a system. The calculated E ads values are ranging from −585 kJ mol−1 (the reduced form of the CC-3N-Mn and a dissociated H2 molecule) to −43 kJ mol−1 (the oxidized form of the CC-3N-Mn). These results suggest that an application of the external electric potential may present a way to achieve the H2 release-and-capture cycle in similar Mn–N-doped graphene materials. On the contrary, the H2 adsorption on the CC-3N-Cr and CC-3N-Fe is less affected by the redox changes of a system. Still, the oxidation and/or reduction of these systems can be used to further improve their H2 storage performance.
